title: Dog Stiff Neck: Why Your Dog Is Keeping Their Head Lowered
meta_desc: Is your dog holding their head low? Learn about stiff neck causes, urgency levels, and how to help your vet triage the situation effectively.
content_html:
What is a Stiff Neck in Dogs?

When a dog keeps their head lowered and refuses to look up or turn, it is usually a sign of cervical pain. This stiffness is a protective posture used to minimize movement in the neck. Common causes range from simple muscle strains to more serious conditions like Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), which involves the cushioning discs between the vertebrae pressing on the spinal cord.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a stiff neck is classified as Medium. While it might not require an ER visit this very second, your dog should be seen by a veterinarian within 24 hours. Because neck pain can quickly escalate into neurological issues, it is important to limit your dog's activity and avoid any jumping or stair-climbing until they are evaluated. If you notice your dog dragging their paws, yelping when touched, or being unable to walk, seek emergency care immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a short video of your dog's posture and movement is incredibly valuable for veterinary triage. A video allows a professional to see the exact angle of the head and check for subtle signs of ataxia (wobbliness) or 'knuckling' of the paws. These visual cues help the veterinary team determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen and which diagnostic tests may be required upon arrival.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human pain relief medication?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your dog human medications like Ibuprofen or Naproxen, as these are highly toxic to dogs. Only use medications specifically prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: Should I use a collar or a harness if my dog has neck pain?
faqs.answer: Switch to a harness immediately. A collar puts direct pressure on the neck and cervical spine, which can significantly worsen the pain or the underlying injury.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of IVDD in the neck?
faqs.answer: Initial signs include a lowered head, shivering or trembling from pain, a tense abdomen, and a reluctance to move or look up toward food bowls or treats.
